A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Hubert Dreyfus

1929 – 2017

Embodied skill, world-involvement, and a critique of representational AI.

I am treating “Dreyfus” as Hubert L. Dreyfus. That is the Dreyfus whose work directly concerns Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, artificial intelligence, skillful coping, mind, consciousness, God, religion, body and soul. Closer to Truth’s profile identifies him as a UC Berkeley philosopher of phenomenology, existentialism, philosophy of psychology and literature, Heidegger interpretation, cognitive science, and AI critique; the same archive lists interviews with him on consciousness, God, creation, religion, body and soul, existence, and meaning.

Central reconstruction

Dreyfus does not offer a conventional “philosophy of mind” in the analytic format: he does not begin with mental states, qualia, propositional attitudes, personal identity, and the mind-body problem as discrete puzzles. He tries to show that this whole way of setting up the problem is already distorted by a Cartesian inheritance. The mind is not, first of all, an inner arena containing representations of an outer world. Human mindedness is embodied, skillful, affectively attuned, socially formed, historically situated openness to a meaningful world. Consciousness is not primarily an inner stream inspected by a subject; it is, at its most basic, the non-thematic openness in which a situation matters and solicits action. “Soul,” if taken to mean an immaterial substance lodged inside the body, is not a Dreyfusian category. But if “soul” means the depth of a human being’s attunement, commitment, vulnerability, and receptivity to what is not self-produced, then Dreyfus’s later theological and existential work is indispensable rather than peripheral.

A concise formula would be: Dreyfus replaces the soul-as-inner-substance and the mind-as-representational-interface with the human being as an embodied site of world-disclosure. The theological supplement is this: the world disclosed is not merely a neutral physical environment; it can shine, solicit, claim, command, enchant, or abandon us. That is why his discussions of God, the sacred, nihilism, polytheism, and Christian or post-Christian meaning belong inside the philosophy of mind, not beside it.

The texts that matter most

The first major source is Dreyfus’s critique of artificial intelligence, especially What Computers Can’t Do and the revised What Computers Still Can’t Do. Google Books summarizes the 1992 MIT Press edition as a revision of the 1972 book and notes that Dreyfus attacked “good old-fashioned AI,” the symbolic-representational project that tried to produce general intelligence from formal symbolic representations.

The second source is his Heideggerian and Merleau-Pontian work: Being-in-the-World, Skillful Coping, Background Practices, “Overcoming the Myth of the Mental,” “Intelligence without Representation,” and “Refocusing the Question: Can There Be Skillful Coping without Propositional Representations or Brain Representations?” These works develop his positive account of mind as skillful, embodied coping rather than symbolic rule-following.

The third source is his anti-mediational realism with Charles Taylor, especially Retrieving Realism. There Dreyfus and Taylor argue against the Cartesian picture in which knowledge consists of ideas in the mind representing the world; instead, they affirm direct contact with physical and social reality and our shared understanding of it.

The fourth source is his existential-theological work: All Things Shining with Sean Dorrance Kelly, “Saving the Sacred from the Axial Revolution,” his Berkeley course descriptions on the Judeo-Christian God, Kierkegaard, Dostoevsky, Nietzsche, and nihilism, and his Closer to Truth conversations about consciousness, God, body, soul, and meaning.

1. The first target: the Cartesian picture of mind

Dreyfus’s deepest opponent is not “AI” narrowly understood. It is a picture of the human being: a subject inside itself, receiving input from an outside world, forming inner representations, applying rules, then issuing behavior. In Retrieving Realism, Dreyfus and Taylor reject this “mediational” picture, the idea that knowledge reaches the world only through mental intermediaries. The NDPR review of Retrieving Realism frames the book as a rejection of the mind as the “mirror of nature,” and as an argument that human representational abilities presuppose prior non-representational engagement with the world.

The German reception of Retrieving Realism makes this point especially clearly. A taz review describes Dreyfus and Taylor as arguing against Descartes’s separation of mind and body and against an “inside-outside” structure of cognition. The review says their alternative emphasizes bodily exchange with the world, everyday coping, background understanding, and even the “coproduction” of world-understanding by human being and world.

This matters for the soul/mind question because Dreyfus thinks the philosophical tradition has repeatedly misdescribed human beings by starting from the wrong abstraction. If one begins with an isolated subject and asks how it reaches the world, one is already late. The human being is always already involved: using equipment, responding to others, navigating significance, acting before theorizing. A theory of mind must therefore begin not with “inner mental contents,” but with being-in-the-world.

2. The second target: representationalism

Dreyfus’s critique of AI is the practical test case for his critique of representationalism. Classical AI assumed that intelligent behavior could be produced by symbol manipulation: represent the world, encode rules, compute outputs. Dreyfus argued that this model mistakes detached, explicit, rule-governed reasoning for the whole of intelligence. In the opening of What Computers Can’t Do, he traces this dream back to Greek rationalism: the hope that reasoning might be reduced to calculation and that practical judgment could be captured by explicit rules.

His objection is not that human intelligence is spooky, supernatural, or outside the physical universe. The preface to What Computers Can’t Do explicitly characterizes Dreyfus as rejecting the view that “man and mind” are set apart from the physical universe, and it quotes his assumption that an artificial embodied agent might be possible in principle if constructed with components sufficiently like those of a human being. The question, for him, is whether such an agent can be made by a digital computer operating under the classical assumptions of formal symbol manipulation.

The philosophical problem is relevance. Human beings do not first encounter neutral facts and then add meaning. We encounter situations already organized by purposes, habits, bodily capacities, social practices, moods, and concerns. Dreyfus argues that if human intelligence is correctly described phenomenologically, there are principled reasons why classical AI cannot be completed: there are no fixed facts sufficient for intelligence because human beings produce facts within changing practices, needs, and forms of life.

A French history of “GOFAI” makes the same point in non-Anglophone reception. Henri Stephanou writes that Dreyfus attacked the computational hypothesis at its core: the idea that intelligence is a formal system instantiated in the world. Dreyfus mobilized existentialist ideas of situation to show that intelligence reasons from context and from a “corps propre,” a lived body. The same article notes that later cognitive science developed connectionist, dynamicist, extended, situated, embodied, and enactive alternatives, while contemporary AI often differs from the 1950s–1970s project Dreyfus originally criticized.

This yields the first extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 1: Mind is not primarily representation. Representation is a derivative achievement of an already embodied agent in an already meaningful world.

3. Intentionality: from inner aiming to being-in-the-world

Dreyfus’s philosophy of mind turns on a transformed account of intentionality. In the Husserl-Searle tradition, intentionality is often described as the mind’s directedness toward objects under conditions of satisfaction. Dreyfus’s Heideggerian response is that the more basic form of intentionality is not an inner mental act pointing toward an object, but practical involvement in a meaningful situation.

A French summary of Dreyfus’s 1993 article on Heidegger, Husserl, and Searle states the contrast with unusual precision: Husserl’s conception of intentionality is tied to representation, while Heidegger’s is tied to being-in-the-world. The same summary says Heidegger criticizes the Husserlian/Searlean approach because the experience of action need not require that I represent myself as causing the action, nor that I logically represent the action’s conditions of satisfaction in order to carry it out.

This is central. When I reach for a cup, avoid a puddle, modulate my tone in conversation, or adjust my balance while walking downstairs, I am not normally representing objects, properties, rules, and goals. The situation is directly intelligible as something-to-be-done. The cup is graspable, the puddle avoidable, the conversation delicate, the step too steep. These are not neutral objects plus mental interpretation. They show up already as affordances or solicitations.

This yields the second extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 2: Basic intentionality is practical, bodily, and world-involving before it is mental, representational, or propositional.

4. Embodiment: the body is not a container for mind

Dreyfus’s positive philosophy of mind is impossible without Merleau-Ponty. In “Intelligence without Representation,” Dreyfus explicitly uses Merleau-Ponty’s notions of the intentional arc and maximal grip. The Springer abstract says the intentional arc names the tight body-world connection through which acquired skills are not stored as representations in the mind but as dispositions to respond to the solicitations of situations. Maximal grip names the body’s tendency to refine its response toward an optimal gestalt.

This is not crude behaviorism. Dreyfus is not saying we are stimulus-response machines. The embodied agent is normatively sensitive: the situation feels balanced or unbalanced, fluent or awkward, inviting or repelling. The body is intelligent because it is already attuned to a field of significance. A tennis player, surgeon, dancer, driver, cook, or speaker responds to a patterned field in which better and worse moves are immediately felt before they are explicitly judged.

The Spanish-language McDowell-Dreyfus debate captures this well. Juan Pablo Hernández Betancur explains that, for Dreyfus, expert non-reflective action is governed by a normativity different from explicitly rational action. Its motivation lies in the Merleau-Pontian tendency toward “agarre máximo,” maximum grip: the reduction of tension or imbalance in order to remain immersed in the flow of the situation.

This yields the third extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 3: The body is not an object moved by a mind; the lived body is the primary medium of mind’s access to significance.

5. Skillful coping: intelligence before rules

Dreyfus’s most famous positive concept is skillful coping. The NDPR review of Skillful Coping says the volume presents Dreyfus’s account of human understanding and practical intelligence as an extended argument against a representationalist account of mind and action. On the representationalist view, knowledge consists of mental representations mediating subject and world, and action is guided by represented beliefs, desires, goals, and rules. Dreyfus disputes almost all of this: learning may use rules, but expertise increasingly leaves rules and representations behind.

This is the familiar Dreyfus model of skill acquisition. The novice begins with rules. The advanced beginner starts recognizing salient aspects. The competent agent chooses and commits. The proficient agent sees the situation holistically. The expert responds directly and fluently. The point is not that experts cannot give reasons afterward, nor that they never deliberate. It is that expert performance as such is not normally produced by deliberation over rules.

“Overcoming the Myth of the Mental” makes the same claim at the level of philosophy of mind. Dreyfus asks whether philosophers can describe the “conceptual upper floors” of knowledge while ignoring the embodied coping on the “ground floor,” and he argues for attention to nonconceptual embodied coping skills shared with animals and infants, as well as the intuitive understanding of experts.

The phrase “nonconceptual” needs care. Dreyfus does not deny that human beings possess concepts, make judgments, deliberate, explain themselves, and reason. He denies that these are the ground floor. Concepts are powerful, but they are parasitic on a more basic practical familiarity. The detached, reflective subject is a special achievement that emerges from absorbed involvement, especially when things break down.

This yields the fourth extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 4: Human intelligence is grounded in acquired know-how, not in rule-governed mental representation. Explicit reason is real, but derivative.

6. Background practices: mind is social before it is private

Dreyfus’s philosophy of mind is not merely embodied; it is also social and historical. The NDPR review of Background Practices calls background practices arguably Dreyfus’s most important contribution. It summarizes the thesis: habituation into background practices is a necessary condition for the intelligibility of people, objects, and institutions. These practices underlie both Dreyfus’s AI critique and his Heidegger interpretation.

A practice is not just a repeated behavior. It is a shared way of coping that provides norms for what counts as appropriate or inappropriate response. These norms are embedded in tools, institutions, language, and ordinary comportment. A classroom, church, dinner table, chessboard, court of law, laboratory, hospital ward, and family kitchen are worlds of intelligibility because practices already tell participants what counts as relevant, fitting, embarrassing, skillful, rude, sacred, dangerous, or trivial.

This means the mind is not private in the way the Cartesian picture imagines. My ability to think depends on my having been initiated into a public world of distinctions. Even apparently private perception is laden with socially inherited salience. I see a traffic light, not a colored luminous object; a wedding ring, not just a metal loop; an altar, not just a table; a lab sample, not just a smear.

French reception has pressed Dreyfus hard on this point. In a discussion of Heidegger’s das Man or “the One,” Christian Perrin describes Dreyfus as giving a Wittgensteinian or ontological interpretation: das Man names the impersonal normative authority that guarantees social practices and makes intraworldly beings understandable and usable. Perrin also notes a worry: Dreyfus risks reifying this “One,” even comparing it by analogy to a “philosophical version of God,” because it seems to guarantee intelligibility for beings.

That criticism is important. Dreyfus’s background practices can sometimes sound like a social substitute for transcendental subjectivity or divine order: an impersonal authority that grants intelligibility. The correction is not to discard the concept but to keep it phenomenological. Background practices are not a hidden super-agent; they are the public, historical, embodied way significance is already organized.

This yields the fifth extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 5: A mind is not first private and then social; it becomes minded by being initiated into shared practices that disclose a world.

7. Consciousness: absorbed, reflective, and metaphysically unresolved

Dreyfus’s view of consciousness must be reconstructed carefully because he does not give a full analytic theory of phenomenal consciousness. He is chiefly concerned with the structure of intelligibility, not with a Chalmers-style account of qualia. Still, several points are clear.

First, Dreyfus rejects the idea that consciousness is primarily an inner object or stream inspected by a subject. Our normal condition is not looking inward at mental contents; it is being absorbed in what we are doing. In absorbed coping, reflective self-consciousness recedes. The skilled agent is not unconscious. Rather, the agent is conscious in a non-observational, pre-reflective, world-directed way.

Second, reflective consciousness arises when coping breaks down or when we step back. If the hammer breaks, the dance falters, the sentence sounds wrong, or the moral situation becomes ambiguous, the agent may shift from absorbed coping to detached attention. At that point, objects, rules, beliefs, and explicit decisions become thematic. Dreyfus’s chapter “Why Heideggerian AI Failed” describes equipment as a solicitation to act and says that solicitations make up the situation from which we sometimes step back and disclose entities.

Third, Dreyfus’s consciousness is field-like rather than object-like. In the Spanish discussion of the McDowell-Dreyfus debate, Dreyfus is presented as drawing on Merleau-Ponty’s description of the football player: the field is not first an object with properties; it is a field of forces, openings, directions, and solicitations. This supports Dreyfus’s idea that absorbed action does not involve a detached subject confronting objects but a body immersed in a structured field.

Fourth, Dreyfus does not solve the metaphysical “hard problem.” In Closer to Truth’s “Landscape of Consciousness,” Dreyfus is quoted as saying that nobody knows how third-person matter could produce consciousness and that AI and computers are not helping us understand it.

That remark is not incidental. It marks a boundary. Dreyfus is not a reductive physicalist explaining consciousness by neural computation. Nor is he straightforwardly a substance dualist. His position is closer to phenomenological non-reductivism: he begins from the lived structure of world-disclosure, insists that any scientific explanation must respect that structure, and doubts that computational modeling gives us the right level of analysis. But he leaves the ultimate metaphysical relation between matter and consciousness unresolved.

This yields the sixth extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 6: Consciousness is basically pre-reflective openness to a meaningful field; reflective self-consciousness is derivative; the metaphysical production of consciousness from matter remains unexplained.

8. The self: not an ego-substance, but a style of being-in-the-world

Dreyfus’s self is not a Cartesian ego. It is not a soul-object hidden behind behavior. The self is a pattern of involvement: a style of coping, caring, choosing, failing, responding, and being claimed by possibilities. The human being is not first a thinking thing and then a body; it is an embodied, world-involved being for whom things matter.

This is why Kierkegaard matters to Dreyfus. His lectures and courses return repeatedly to commitment, despair, nihilism, and what it means to have a meaningful life. The Internet Archive description of Dreyfus’s Berkeley course Existentialism in Literature and Film says the course is organized around attempts to reinterpret the Judeo-Christian God and to determine in what sense such a God is still living; it studies Dostoevsky’s and Kierkegaard’s attempts to preserve a non-theological version of the God of Christianity, along with Nietzsche’s attempt to save us from belief in any traditional version of God.

The self, in this existential register, is endangered by detached autonomy. A purely autonomous subject, free to choose meanings at will, risks nihilism because meanings cannot be made binding merely by decision. Dreyfus’s work on the Internet, as cited in Rafael Capurro’s discussion of digital hermeneutics, argues that life on the Web can reduce vulnerability and commitment; if Kierkegaard is right, the lack of passion diminishes meaning.

This yields the seventh extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 7: The self is not an inner observer but a historically formed, embodied way of being committed in a world.

9. Soul: what Dreyfus rejects and what he preserves

Dreyfus does not defend a doctrine of the immortal soul. If “soul” means a separable immaterial substance that contains consciousness and survives independently of embodiment, Dreyfus gives us no such theory. His whole trajectory resists the idea that the essence of the human being is an inner, self-contained mental substance.

But it would be a mistake to conclude that Dreyfus simply dissolves the soul into brain mechanisms. He does not. He rejects both the disembodied soul-substance and the computational brain-machine as adequate accounts of human being. The “soul,” reconstructed in Dreyfusian terms, is better understood as the human being’s depth of attuned openness: the way a life is claimed by practices, commitments, moods, exemplars, sacred moments, and shared worlds.

This interpretation becomes strongest when Dreyfus turns to religion and the sacred. His Berkeley Philosophy 7 course description says that in the traditional Judeo-Christian understanding, God is the ground of all meaning; after the medieval world, Descartes and Kant try to promote autonomous Man into that grounding role; both God and Man as ground fail, leaving the threat of nihilism. The course then asks whether we can preserve the existential insight that life needs some kind of ground without locating that ground in either a Supreme Being or autonomous Man.

That is a philosophy of the soul in existential form. The question is not “where is the soul located?” but “what can ground a life so that it is not empty, arbitrary, or self-enclosed?” Dreyfus’s answer is not traditional metaphysical theism, but neither is it secular voluntarism. The human being needs an authority, claim, or source of meaning that is not simply self-generated.

This yields the eighth extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 8: The soul is not an inner substance; it is the existential depth of a human being’s openness to meaning, commitment, and non-self-originating authority.

10. Theology is not a distraction: the sacred as given meaning

Dreyfus’s theology is best understood as an extension of his anti-Cartesian philosophy of mind. If the mind is not a sovereign inner subject constructing meaning from representations, then meaning is something we receive, discover, inhabit, and respond to. Theology names the most intense forms of this receptivity.

In “Saving the Sacred from the Axial Revolution,” Kelly and Dreyfus argue that a purely rational and secular characterization of human beings leaves something out. They claim that traditional monotheism is sociologically difficult after the “death of God,” but that Homeric polytheism preserves a phenomenologically rich account of the sacred. By opening ourselves to moods of wonder and gratitude, they argue, we can recover intensity and meaningfulness.

In All Things Shining, this is developed as a critique of autonomous meaning-making. An interview with Sean Dorrance Kelly explains that the book is neutral, at least on one interpretation, about whether gods literally exist. What it opposes is the idea that the individual is the only source of meaning. Kelly says that it would be silly simply to assert that Athena literally exists, but that Homer captured a real phenomenon: masterly skillful activity can feel as though excellence is not sourced merely from oneself.

That is the theological counterpart of skillful coping. In expert action, the agent is drawn by the situation; in sacred experience, the person is drawn by a source of significance that feels given. The same structural pattern repeats: anti-autonomy, receptivity, solicitation, attunement, and response.

The Pascalian element is also crucial. Kelly says that even if one is convinced by Pascal’s Wager, one cannot become a believer merely by decision; one must cultivate practices, rituals, and skills that open one to non-identical authority. This is exactly Dreyfus’s skill model transposed into religion: religious receptivity is not merely assent to propositions but initiation into practices that transform what can show up as meaningful.

Dreyfus and Kelly’s “polytheism” should therefore not be read crudely as a return to literal Olympus. It is a phenomenology of plural modes of receptivity. Homer, Dante, Melville, Christianity, civic rhetoric, craftsmanship, sports, music, and moral courage can each disclose different worlds of significance. Kelly explicitly says their position need not require abandoning monotheism; he describes an “open Christianity” in Melville and says they have nothing against monotheism that remains open to admirable goods in other ways of life, while opposing Ahab-like demands for an ultimate unified meaning available now.

This yields the ninth extracted thesis:

Dreyfusian Thesis 9: The sacred is the experience of meaning as given rather than manufactured; theology is a phenomenology of receptivity to non-self-identical authority.

11. Moods, affect, and world-disclosure

Dreyfus’s philosophy of mind cannot be reduced to cognition. It is also affective. Heidegger’s moods and Merleau-Ponty’s bodily tensions are not subjective colorings added to neutral facts; they are ways the world is disclosed. A situation can show up as inviting, threatening, boring, sacred, shameful, radiant, empty, or urgent.

This is why Dreyfus’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of meaning. To be minded is to find oneself in a world where things already matter. A mood is not merely an inner feeling; it is a way the whole field of relevance is configured. Anxiety, boredom, gratitude, wonder, courage, despair, and joy disclose different worlds.

The religious dimension intensifies this. Wonder and gratitude are not decorative feelings but modes in which the world can appear as sacred. In the Dreyfus-Kelly framework, the sacred is not first a doctrine but a lived attunement. This connects directly to Dreyfus’s critique of AI: a system manipulating symbols may process information, but it does not thereby inhabit a world in which possibilities matter, where failure wounds, where excellence shines, where one is answerable to what calls.

12. AI and the mind: what would an artificial mind need?

Dreyfus’s AI critique is often misunderstood as a blanket claim that machines can never do anything impressive. That is not the best reading. His claim is more specific: classical symbolic AI fails because it assumes intelligence can be formalized as rule-governed manipulation of representations detached from embodiment, background, needs, and practices. His own position allows, at least in principle, an artificial embodied agent made from components sufficiently like those of human beings; what he denies is that a purely formal digital-symbolic system captures human intelligence.

Dreyfus’s later article “Why Heideggerian AI Failed and How Fixing It Would Require Making It More Heideggerian” says human understanding involves an intentional arc: actions and projections are drawn out by meaningful features of the world, and those actions in turn alter how the world shows up as soliciting us. This is a dynamic, embodied feedback structure, not a static database.

The French OpenEdition discussion is useful here because it distinguishes historical GOFAI from contemporary AI. It notes that today’s AI often uses statistical machine-learning methods and no longer straightforwardly takes itself as a model of the human mind, while also observing that the computational hypothesis remains a recurring temptation in cognitive science and artificial general intelligence projects.

A Dreyfusian artificial mind would therefore need at least: embodiment, needs, vulnerability, temporality, affective salience, practical purposes, social initiation, background practices, skill acquisition, and openness to significance. It would not be enough to add more facts or more processing power. The question would be whether the system has a world, not merely data.

13. The McDowell challenge: is Dreyfus too anti-conceptual?

The strongest philosophical challenge to Dreyfus comes from John McDowell and related intellectualists. Dreyfus says absorbed coping is nonconceptual; McDowell argues that human action and perception are pervaded by rational and conceptual capacities, even when no explicit deliberation occurs.

The Spanish-language article by Hernández Betancur is valuable because it neither simply dismisses Dreyfus nor repeats the Anglo debate uncritically. It frames the disagreement as intellectualism versus anti-intellectualism and argues that Dreyfus’s anti-intellectualist argument succeeds only if one accepts hidden presuppositions that are not self-evident.

The same article grants Dreyfus’s phenomenological point: expert non-reflective action often responds immediately and skillfully without explicit inference, calculation, or deliberation. But it argues that the phenomenology of nonconceptual solicitation does not rule out another description under which the agent knows what she is doing and why. A solicitation, the author says, can be a reason.

This is a serious correction. Dreyfus is strongest when he denies that expert coping is produced by explicit rules or inner representations. He is weaker if he implies that conceptual rationality is wholly absent from skilled human action. A balanced Dreyfusian philosophy of mind should say: absorbed coping is not caused by conceptual deliberation as its inner mechanism, but human coping can often be rationally articulable because the agent belongs to a conceptually structured form of life.

14. The Heidegger-Wittgenstein problem: is Dreyfus too normativist?

Another important non-English debate concerns Dreyfus’s reading of Heidegger through Wittgensteinian background practices. A French review of Charlotte Gauvry’s Heidegger et Wittgenstein: Contexte, monde ambiant, arrière-plan says the book critically examines a tradition of comparison inaugurated by Dreyfus’s 1990/1991 Heidegger commentary. It aims to clarify the difference between Heidegger’s hermeneutics of facticity and Wittgensteinian contextualism.

This matters because Dreyfus sometimes makes Heidegger sound like a philosopher of social norms and shared practices. That reading is powerful for philosophy of mind and cognitive science, but it may flatten Heidegger’s more ontological concerns: disclosedness, temporality, care, death, anxiety, authenticity, and the question of Being. Perrin’s article on das Man similarly suggests that Dreyfus’s positive, norm-based reading of the One needs supplementation because das Man is also a source of concealment, leveling, and self-loss.

The correction is again constructive. Dreyfus’s background practices should not be reduced to sociological conventions. They are existential-ontological conditions of intelligibility, and they can both disclose and conceal. The “soul” question returns here: a human being can be absorbed in average public meanings and still lose itself. Authenticity is not the rejection of all background practices, but a transformed appropriation of one’s situated possibilities.

15. Technology, the Internet, and disembodied life

Dreyfus’s philosophy of mind also implies a critique of technology. He does not merely ask whether machines can think; he asks what machine-mediated existence does to human meaning. Capurro’s discussion of digital hermeneutics says the story begins with Dreyfus’s critique of AI and his emphasis on everyday practices in which we are embedded before knowledge objectivations and symbolic manipulations. It also quotes Dreyfus’s On the Internet argument that life on the Web can remove vulnerability and commitment, thereby weakening meaning in a Kierkegaardian sense.

This is directly relevant to consciousness and soul. If mindedness depends on embodied involvement, risk, vulnerability, and commitment, then disembodied or frictionless environments can alter the kind of self one becomes. A life of options without binding commitment may increase control while diminishing depth. Dreyfus’s concern is not technophobia; it is that certain technologies reinforce the Cartesian fantasy of detached, invulnerable agency.

16. A systematic Dreyfusian philosophy of mind

Here is the comprehensive philosophy of mind one can extract from Dreyfus.

Ontology. Human beings are not minds inside bodies, nor bodies plus mental software. They are embodied beings-in-the-world. Mind is an achieved openness to a world of significance. Body, world, practice, affect, and understanding are internally related, not externally added.

Epistemology. Knowledge is not primarily accurate inner representation. It is grounded in direct practical contact with reality. Theoretical knowledge is real, but it grows out of pre-theoretical coping. Scientific detachment is a powerful transformation of involvement, not the original form of access.

Intentionality. The basic directedness of mind is not “mental content about an object.” It is practical directedness within a situation. We are drawn, solicited, repelled, guided, and corrected by fields of significance before we formulate judgments.

Perception. Perception is not passive reception of sense-data. It is embodied responsiveness to affordances and solicitations. What is perceived is already organized by bodily capacities and practical concerns.

Action. Action is not normally the execution of represented intentions by bodily machinery. In skilled action, the agent responds fluently to the changing situation. Rules and plans matter especially in learning, teaching, breakdown, and repair.

Conceptual thought. Concepts are not the foundation of all mindedness. They are higher-level articulations made possible by embodied coping and background practices. They allow criticism, explanation, science, ethics, and distance, but they do not float free of the ground floor.

Consciousness. Consciousness is not basically an inner object. It is world-involving openness. Absorbed coping is conscious without being self-observational. Reflective consciousness emerges when the agent steps back. The hard metaphysical question of how matter produces consciousness remains unresolved.

Selfhood. The self is not an inner ego-substance. It is a historically formed style of being-in-the-world, shaped by practices, commitments, moods, and possibilities. Authenticity is not pure self-creation; it is a transformed relation to the possibilities one inherits.

Soul. Dreyfus does not defend the soul as an immaterial substance. But he preserves what the soul was often meant to name: the depth of a life, the possibility of despair or commitment, the vulnerability to meaning, the capacity to be claimed by something beyond self-will.

The sacred. The sacred is not an optional religious topic appended to his philosophy. It is the existential intensification of his theory of meaning. Just as skilled action is drawn by solicitations, sacred life is drawn by shining sources of significance. Theology, for Dreyfus, is a way of articulating the fact that the deepest meanings are received, cultivated, and inhabited rather than manufactured by autonomous choice.

AI. A computational system may simulate many behaviors, but Dreyfus’s challenge is whether it has embodied needs, background practices, affective attunement, vulnerability, and a world. Intelligence is not just problem-solving; it is situated coping in a meaningful world.

17. Final assessment

Dreyfus’s philosophy of mind is powerful because it changes the starting point. Instead of asking how a mind inside the head represents the world outside, he asks how an embodied human being is already involved in a world where things matter. From that shift, his views on AI, skill, perception, action, consciousness, selfhood, technology, and religion become parts of one system.

The system is not complete. It does not give a full metaphysics of phenomenal consciousness, a doctrine of life after death, a detailed neuroscience, or a precise theory of when conceptual capacities enter skilled action. It also faces serious objections from McDowell-style conceptualists, from critics of Dreyfus’s Heidegger-Wittgenstein synthesis, and from those who think contemporary AI has changed the terrain. But as a philosophical anthropology of mind, it is unusually unified.

The best name for the extracted view is:

embodied phenomenological realism with a theology of given meaning.

Or, more fully:

Human mindedness is the embodied, skilled, affectively attuned, socially initiated, historically situated, and sometimes sacredly claimed disclosure of a meaningful world; consciousness is a mode of this disclosure, reflective thought is a derivative articulation of it, and the soul is not an inner substance but the depth of a life’s openness to meaning beyond autonomous self-production.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
